abridged.content:
The cardiac cycle - one beat as pressure-driven valve movement

The cardiac cycle is one complete heartbeat: ventricular filling, atrial contribution, ventricular contraction, ventricular ejection, ventricular relaxation, and refilling. It is triggered electrically by the ECG, but the bedside physiology is mechanical. Valves open and close because pressure gradients change, not because the ECG directly opens them.

Diastole is the filling phase. It begins after aortic and pulmonary valve closure and includes isovolumetric relaxation, early rapid ventricular filling, diastasis, and atrial systole. Systole is the contraction and ejection phase. It begins after mitral and tricuspid valve closure and includes isovolumetric contraction and ventricular ejection.

During diastole, the mitral and tricuspid valves open once ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure. During systole, the AV valves close once ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure. The aortic and pulmonary valves open once ventricular pressure exceeds aortic or pulmonary artery pressure. They close when ventricular pressure falls below arterial pressure. The dicrotic notch on the arterial waveform represents aortic valve closure.

In CTICU, the cardiac cycle explains why rhythm, rate, preload, afterload, contractility, valve function, pacing, tamponade, ventilation, and IABP timing all matter. An organised ECG rhythm does not guarantee useful mechanical output. The arterial waveform, pulse pressure, CVP, PA pressures, cardiac index, lactate, urine output, echo, and venous saturation tell you whether each beat is producing effective forward flow.

expanded.content:
The cardiac cycle - electrical trigger, mechanical pressure event

The cardiac cycle is best understood as a sequence of pressure gradients. The ECG is important because it initiates atrial and ventricular depolarisation, but the valves respond to pressure, not directly to electricity. This distinction is essential in CTICU because patients can have an apparently organised electrical rhythm while mechanical output is poor.

A paced QRS, sinus rhythm, or narrow-complex rhythm does not prove that the ventricle is filling adequately, generating enough pressure, ejecting effectively, or relaxing properly. Mechanical output depends on preload, afterload, contractility, rhythm timing, AV synchrony, valve function, ventricular compliance, and intrathoracic pressure.

Late diastole and atrial systole

Late diastole occurs when the ventricles are relaxed and filling. The mitral and tricuspid valves are open because atrial pressure exceeds ventricular pressure. The aortic and pulmonary valves are closed because arterial pressure exceeds ventricular pressure. Most ventricular filling is passive. Atrial systole then provides the final active contribution to ventricular filling, commonly called the atrial kick.

Atrial kick is especially important when the ventricle is stiff. In a compliant ventricle, passive filling may be enough. In a hypertrophied LV after aortic stenosis, a post-bypass oedematous ventricle, a diastolic dysfunction patient, or a valve surgery patient, atrial contribution can be critical. New atrial fibrillation removes organised atrial contraction and often shortens filling time because the ventricular rate becomes fast.

The result of losing atrial contribution may be sudden reduction in stroke volume, pulse pressure, MAP, urine output, and SvO₂. This is why new post-operative AF is not just an arrhythmia label. It is a mechanical filling problem.

Isovolumetric contraction

After ventricular depolarisation, seen as the QRS complex, ventricular pressure rises. When LV pressure exceeds LA pressure, the mitral valve closes. When RV pressure exceeds RA pressure, the tricuspid valve closes. This produces S1. At this point all valves are closed, so ventricular volume does not change even though pressure rises.

This phase is called isovolumetric contraction. The LV must build enough pressure to exceed aortic diastolic pressure before the aortic valve opens. The RV must build enough pressure to exceed pulmonary artery pressure before the pulmonary valve opens. This phase reflects pressure-generating capacity.

Poor contractility, acidosis, hypothermia, hypocalcaemia, myocardial stunning, ischaemia, or excessive afterload can all make pressure generation slower or weaker. Clinically, poor pressure generation may appear as a weak or slow arterial upstroke once ejection begins, narrow pulse pressure, low cardiac index, cool peripheries, rising lactate, low SvO₂, and increasing vasoactive support.

Ventricular ejection

Ventricular ejection begins when LV pressure exceeds aortic pressure and the aortic valve opens. On the right side, it begins when RV pressure exceeds pulmonary artery pressure and the pulmonary valve opens. The AV valves remain closed. Blood is ejected into the aorta and pulmonary artery.

The arterial upstroke is a bedside expression of ventricular ejection. A brisk upstroke suggests effective ejection and/or low impedance. A slow-rising upstroke suggests low LV contractility, severe aortic stenosis physiology, low stroke volume, excessive afterload, or an overdamped arterial line.

Stroke volume is produced during ejection, but it is determined by what happened before and during ejection: preload, contractility, and afterload. An underfilled ventricle ejects little because it starts with too little volume. A stunned ventricle ejects poorly because it cannot generate enough force. A severely afterloaded ventricle ejects poorly because the resistance is too high. A compressed ventricle in tamponade ejects poorly because filling is mechanically restricted.

Aortic valve closure and isovolumetric relaxation

As ventricular contraction ends, LV pressure falls. When LV pressure falls below aortic pressure, the aortic valve closes. This generates the dicrotic notch on the arterial waveform and contributes to S2. The pulmonary valve closes similarly on the right side.

After semilunar valve closure, all valves are again closed. Ventricular pressure falls without a change in volume. This is isovolumetric relaxation. Once LV pressure falls below LA pressure, the mitral valve opens and LV filling begins. Once RV pressure falls below RA pressure, the tricuspid valve opens and RV filling begins.

Diastole is clinically active, not passive downtime. Most LV coronary perfusion occurs during diastole because intramyocardial pressure compresses coronary vessels during systole. Tachycardia shortens diastole disproportionately. Therefore, a heart rate of 130-150/min in a fresh CABG, valve, or stiff-ventricle patient can reduce LV filling, reduce coronary perfusion time, increase myocardial oxygen demand, and lower stroke volume.

Valve timing summary

Cycle phaseMitral/tricuspid valvesAortic/pulmonary valvesMain event
Late diastoleOpenClosedPassive filling plus atrial systole.
Isovolumetric contractionClosedClosedVentricular pressure rises without ejection.
Ventricular ejectionClosedOpenBlood is ejected into aorta and pulmonary artery.
Isovolumetric relaxationClosedClosedVentricular pressure falls after semilunar valve closure.
Early fillingOpenClosedVentricles refill once pressure falls below atrial pressure.

CTICU interpretation of cycle failure

Different clinical problems impair different parts of the cycle. Hypovolaemia, tachycardia, tamponade, high intrathoracic pressure, and RV failure impair filling. Myocardial stunning, ischaemia, hypothermia, acidosis, hypocalcaemia, and excessive afterload impair pressure generation and ejection. Valve obstruction impairs forward ejection. Regurgitant lesions waste stroke volume by allowing backward flow.

The monitor rarely labels the failing phase. You infer it from the pattern. Falling MAP with rising CVP and narrowing pulse pressure after cardiac surgery suggests obstructive or RV physiology until proven otherwise. Low MAP with warm peripheries, wide pulse pressure, and high CI suggests vasoplegia. Low CI with high SVR suggests pressure without flow. New rapid AF with falling pulse pressure suggests loss of filling and diastolic time.

The key CTICU skill is to identify which part of the cycle is failing and why. Treating the number without identifying the mechanism can worsen the patient. Fluid helps if the ventricle is preload responsive and tolerant. Inotropy helps if contractility is inadequate. Vasopressor helps if vascular tone is too low. Afterload reduction helps if resistance is excessive. Surgical escalation is required if tamponade, graft failure, valve obstruction, or bleeding is the cause.

pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls - The Cardiac Cycle

Pearl 1 - The ECG is not the circulation

An organised ECG rhythm does not prove effective mechanical output. A paced QRS or sinus rhythm can exist with poor filling, poor ejection, tamponade, RV failure, severe afterload, or low stroke volume.

Pearl 2 - Valves are pressure-gradient devices

Mitral and tricuspid valves close when ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure. Aortic and pulmonary valves open when ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure.

Pearl 3 - The dicrotic notch is aortic valve closure

The dicrotic notch is useful for arterial waveform interpretation and IABP timing. If it is absent or distorted, check low flow, damping, low SVR, artefact, and aortic valve physiology.

Pearl 4 - Diastole is where filling and LV coronary perfusion happen

Tachycardia shortens diastole and can reduce ventricular filling and coronary perfusion. This is especially dangerous in CABG, valve, hypertrophied LV, stunned myocardium, and RV failure patients.

Pearl 5 - Atrial kick is not optional in stiff ventricles

New AF after cardiac surgery can sharply reduce stroke volume because atrial systole is lost and diastolic filling time shortens.

equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In - The Cardiac Cycle

Arterial line

The arterial line displays the pressure consequence of ventricular ejection. The upstroke reflects LV ejection. The systolic peak reflects stroke volume, contractility, arterial compliance, and vascular tone. The dicrotic notch reflects aortic valve closure.

FindingCycle meaningPossible causeImmediate check
Slow upstrokeDelayed or weak ejectionLow contractility, low SV, aortic stenosis, high afterload, overdampingCheck pulse, damping, transducer, CO/CI, echo, valve history
Narrow pulse pressureReduced stroke volumeHypovolaemia, tamponade, LV failure, RV failure, high afterload, dampingCheck drains, CVP trend, lactate, UO, echo, line quality
Wide pulse pressureHigh SV or low diastolic resistanceVasoplegia, aortic regurgitation, high output, underdampingCheck diastolic pressure, SVR, warmth, flush test
Absent notchAortic closure not clearly transmittedLow flow, damping, low SVR, artefact, valve issueCheck waveform, square wave test, NIBP correlation

Pacing boxes

Temporary pacing changes the timing of atrial and ventricular activation. AAI preserves atrial timing if AV conduction is intact. VVI maintains ventricular rate but may lose atrial contribution. DDD attempts to coordinate atrial and ventricular timing.

IABP

The IABP must be timed to the cardiac cycle. Inflation should occur at the dicrotic notch after aortic valve closure. Deflation should occur just before systole.

Echo

Echo shows chamber filling, contraction, valve movement, septal shift, RV dilation, LV underfilling, valve lesions, and pericardial compression.

targets.content:
Targets and Numbers - The Cardiac Cycle

The cardiac cycle is managed by ensuring that each beat has enough filling time, coordinated atrial and ventricular timing, adequate ventricular pressure generation, effective ejection, and enough diastolic time for coronary perfusion. There is no single cardiac-cycle target. The pattern across rate, rhythm, MAP, pulse pressure, CI, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation determines whether the cycle is effective.

tables.heading: Cardiac cycle bedside parameters
rows: Heart rate
Usually 60-100/min; often 70-90/min if stable post-op
Determines cycle frequency and filling time
Too slow may reduce CO; too fast may reduce filling and coronary perfusion
Rhythm
Sinus rhythm preferred when atrial contribution is important
Coordinates atrial systole before ventricular systole
New AF can remove atrial kick and reduce stroke volume
MAP
Often 65-85 mmHg
Pressure generated by flow and vascular tone
Normal MAP does not prove adequate flow
Pulse pressure
Often 30-50 mmHg, context dependent
Stroke volume and arterial compliance clue
Narrowing suggests low SV or damping
Cardiac index
≥2.2 L/min/m² minimum practical target
Repeated cycles producing flow
Interpret with lactate, UO, SvO₂, skin perfusion

quizQuestions.question: What does the dicrotic notch on an arterial waveform represent?
quizQuestions.explanation: The dicrotic notch represents aortic valve closure as LV pressure falls below aortic pressure. It marks the end of ventricular ejection and helps with arterial waveform interpretation and IABP timing.

quizQuestions.question: Why can new rapid AF after valve surgery cause hypotension?
options: Loss of atrial kick and shortened diastolic filling time reduce stroke volume
quizQuestions.explanation: Rapid AF removes coordinated atrial systole and shortens diastole. In stiff post-operative ventricles this reduces filling and stroke volume.

quizQuestions.question: A paced ECG at 80/min is present but pulse pressure is narrow, lactate is rising, and SvO₂ is falling. What is the safest interpretation?
options: Electrical activity is organised but mechanical output may be inadequate
Pacing spikes prove stroke volume is normal
Narrow pulse pressure always means underdamping
quizQuestions.explanation: Electrical rhythm does not guarantee mechanical output. Narrow pulse pressure, rising lactate, and falling SvO₂ suggest inadequate stroke volume or oxygen delivery.
